title: The value of privacy and the choice of limited partners by venture capitalists
abstract: We study how information disclosure concerns shape the choice of limited partners (LPs) by venture capitalists (VCs). Late-2002 court rulings prevented public LPs from providing confidentiality to investment managers. The best-performing VCs, but not other managers, responded by excluding public LPs from their new funds. Lost access reduced public LP returns by $1.6 billion relative to $14 billion of their VC commitments. Legislation reducing disclosure, contracts limiting information access, and added fund-of-funds intermediaries helped restore access. These changes focused on protecting portfolio company information, highlighting the importance of proprietary information for VC investing and the potential costs of transparency.

article.1. Introduction: After 22 years of being in business together, forces entirely beyond our control have impelled us to end our relationship. 1Michael Moritz, letter to the University of California, August 2003.Companies accessing public markets trade off the benefits of raising capital with the costs of making the information publicly available (Bhattacharya and Ritter, 1983, Doidge et al., 2017). Venture capital (VC) funds face a similar trade-off when accepting capital from state pension plans and public university endowments. These public investors constitute one of the largest pools of institutional money, but that capital comes with a disclosure cost, as these investors are subject to disclosure requirements under the state-level Freedom of Information Acts (FOIAs). While the allocation of public investors to VC has dramatically increased over the last two decades, little is known about the impact of disclosure requirements on this asset class. Understanding the consequences of these laws both informs the ongoing policy debate about disclosure regulation and sheds light on the unique features of the VC industry.2Although VC funds are opaque to outsiders, there is extensive information sharing between VCs and the limited partners (LPs) that invest into VC funds. A major driver of this is LPs’ need to evaluate VC skill. More so than most asset managers, like, for example, those managing mutual funds (Fama and French, 2010) and buyout (BO) funds (Kaplan and Schoar, 2005), some VC fund managers appear to be able to generate persistent excess returns (Harris et al., 2023). Beyond that, information helps VCs build trust and allows LPs to manage the illiquidity and uncertainty inherent in private market investments (Nadauld et al., 2019).This information sharing might start with a pitch outlining the VC fund’s team, the fund’s investment thesis, the fund’s structure, the performance of the firm’s past funds, and the status of any prior investments. An LP’s subsequent due diligence and investment decision would generate further information flow, including a limited partnership agreement (LPA) detailing the fund terms. Such terms include the fund’s fees, the fund’s governance structure, and the LP’s information rights, with major LPs being able to negotiate these terms either on behalf of all LPs or for only themselves through a so-called side letter. After initial capital calls, the fund would provide its LPs, on an ongoing basis, formal financial documents that outline the fund’s asset values, cash flows, and performance metrics. In addition to fund-level data, these reports specify the performance of companies in the fund’s portfolio. VCs consider all this information confidential.In this paper, we study how disclosure concerns impact the matching between VCs and their LPs. Specifically, we exploit exogenous variation in LPs’ ability to provide confidentiality. This variation is a result of court rulings in late 2002, which built on state-level FOIAs to force public LPs to disclose the performance of their private equity (PE) investments (including VC funds) to the public. Originally enacted over the course of the 20th century, state-level FOIAs, also known as sunshine laws, force government bodies to disclose some types of information in response to requests from third parties. These laws aim to balance the costs and benefits of transparency by exempting trade secrets and other sensitive information from FOIA requests. However, the scope of these exemptions is often unclear and is determined in part by court cases. Following the dot-com crash and the Enron scandal, journalists and public employees used these acts to initiate lawsuits seeking access to the information that fund managers provide to their public investors. High-profile initial court rulings in late 2002 forced the disclosure of the fund-level return data and left the secrecy of portfolio company information and other sensitive data as an open question. These rulings meant VC funds with public LPs could be forced to make more information public.The fact that VCs voluntarily disclosed essentially none of these data to the public suggests there are costs to disclosure. However, it is ex-ante unclear how agents (VCs) would react to increased information disclosure requirements imposed on their principals (public LPs). One hypothesis is that VCs with certain focus areas, such as those targeting certain industries or early-stage startups, would value confidentiality more and react more strongly. Alternatively, poorly performing VCs might show the largest reaction, as they could lose the most from publicizing their (subpar) returns. Along those lines, consistent with the evidence on misallocation from Hochberg and Rauh (2012), transparency might discipline public LPs out of poorly performing investments. A third scenario is that the VC-LP matches are sticky and would not change after the FOIA shock, due to, for example, an informational hold-up preventing VCs from easily replacing their existing LP base (Hochberg et al., 2014).To test these hypotheses, we use Preqin data on LP commitments to VC funds. We split U.S.-based LPs into public and private LPs, with public LPs being subject to FOIAs and private LPs serving as a natural control group. Our public LPs are mostly state pension plans and public university endowments, while private LPs include private pension plans, foundations, and private endowments. We consider a variety of VC characteristics, including fund size; the fund’s target stage, geography, industry; and VC firm quality. We measure VC firm quality by ranking VC firms based on the number of IPOs VCs had in their portfolios (with board seats) between 1995 and 2001 in VentureSource data (similar to Nahata (2008)). We classify the top 20 firms as “top VCs”, which collectively accounted for 9% of funds and 22% of raised capital over that period.We first focus on how the fraction of commitments by public LPs changed after the court decisions, which we refer to as the FOIA shock. Following the FOIA shock, the best-performing VCs dramatically reduced their commitments from public LPs relative to a control group of nontop VCs: the fraction of public investments dropped by 15 percentage points, about one-half of the pre-shock values. Other VC characteristics, such as stage, industry focus, and target geography, are not associated with a differential change in public LP share. The dynamics show pre-shock parallel trends and an immediate, differential response, supporting our causal interpretation. While anecdotal accounts on the response of VC firms make it clear that many VCs disliked the FOIA rulings, we show that, on average, only top VCs reacted by excluding public LPs from their funds.Next, we turn to the LP-level analysis to study changes in the aggregate portfolio composition of investors’ private market allocations. We confirm that following the FOIA shock public LPs reduced their allocation to top VC funds and reallocated to funds of funds (FoFs) with established top VC relationships: intermediaries that might allow them to regain exposure to the best-performing VCs. This reallocation is consistent with the idea that, by creating an additional layer of reporting where the private intermediary does not pass along the portfolio company information received from its GPs to its LPs, FoFs help public investors maintain their exposure to best-performing VCs. This broad pattern is consistent with case studies and anecdotal evidence from public endowments.Using data from MSCI-Burgiss, we quantify the impact of these reallocations on public LPs’ returns in the 2003–2006 period — immediately following the FOIA shock. We estimate the effect of the FOIA shock by comparing public LPs’ actual returns with the returns they would have received by following a simple reinvestment strategy. In light of the FoF reallocations discussed above, we consider the returns to public LPs’ combined commitments in VC and FoF, which accounted for $14 billion and $12 billion, respectively. Compared to the naive reinvestment counterfactual, public LPs’ exposure to the best-performing VC firms was halved, reducing their returns by $4.3 billion. The reallocations within nontop VCs and usage of FoFs to regain access to top VCs helped public LPs offset a large part of this performance gap, bringing the forgone returns to $1.6 billion overall (6% on $26 billion of VC and FoF investments combined).Our results show that only the best-performing VCs reduced their commitments from public LPs. Given that forced disclosure could adversely affect all VCs and the companies they back, it is perhaps puzzling why only top VCs reduced their public commitments. We attempt to differentiate between two potential drivers of this response. First, top VCs could have a greater ability to replace LPs by virtue of having excess LP demand (Sensoy et al., 2014) – we call this a differential supply of capital channel. Second, top VCs could care more about privacy than other VCs – for example, top VCs could have better-quality proprietary information on portfolio companies or a stronger desire to conceal fee discounts given to some investors – we label this a differential demand for privacy channel. Importantly, VCs having an underlying demand for privacy drives the effect in both cases; what distinguishes these two channels is whether the difference between top VCs and nontop VCs is driven by top VCs having a stronger demand for privacy than nontop VCs or by top VCs having a greater ability to act on that desire than nontop VCs.We find stronger evidence supporting the supply of capital channel. First, the results of our tests of VC characteristics speak against the demand-side arguments: while both early-stage funds and funds in particular industries might have higher demands for privacy, neither stage nor industry is associated with VCs’ response to the disclosure shock. Second, a demand for privacy driven by the desire to conceal the fee-based discrimination among LPs is unlikely. On the contrary, as Begenau and Siriwardane (2024) document, funds in high demand are less likely to use a tiered fee structure, suggesting that top VCs tend not to segregate their LPs using different fee schedules. Third, we reestimate our main results based on a sample of BO firms, which are, in general, more scalable than VC firms (Metrick and Yasuda, 2010). The ability to expand fund sizes means that BO firms do not face the LP rationing dynamic that characterizes top VCs. Similar to top VC funds, the best-performing BO funds could also arguably possess more valuable proprietary information than their nontop counterparts. However, top BO funds show little to no reaction to the disclosure laws, casting further doubt on the demand-side argument. Finally, we see a similar reduction in public commitments when we look at other proxies of VC firms that had scaled closer to their optimal capacity, such as above-standard high carried interest, presence of auxiliary funds, and fund sequence number. Although we cannot rule out a differential demand for privacy, the presented evidence is more consistent with the supply of capital being the reason why only top VCs appear to actively respond to the FOIA shock.Our causal mechanism is supported by our tests on top VC allocations, direct quotes from VCs and LPs, and the apparent reallocation of public LPs into FoFs with top VC exposure. However, there may be a lingering concern that the remnants of the dot-com bubble burst contaminate our results. We address these concerns in two ways. First, we show that there was no decrease in public LPs’ allocation to top VCs in the period after the dot-com burst but before the FOIA shock. Second, our results persist at the fund level, commitment level, and LP level, in addition to passing a number of other robustness checks. Varying the level of analysis allows us to control for a host of parameters, including LP identity, VC firm identity, and time-variant LP state characteristics.Given that VCs appear extremely sensitive to disclosure, it is natural to ask exactly what type of information they seek to protect. We shed light on this question by analyzing LPAs of funds raised by the same VC firms immediately before and after the FOIA shock . We document the emergence of several strategies GPs use to protect information from FOIA disclosure, including limiting the information released to public LPs and providing information to them only through temporary online portals. VCs overwhelmingly added strong protections for portfolio company information. While return information was protected by some VCs, other VCs explicitly permitted disclosure if required by law. The contrast between these two types of sensitive information suggests that both VCs and LPs agreed that portfolio company information should remain private.Both publicly and privately sourced datasets in venture capital echo this wedge between portfolio company and fund financial information: while the data on portfolio companies are generally incomplete, we know substantially more about fund-level performance (Kaplan and Lerner, 2016). In addition, the datasets relying on public information may miss the return information of leading VC firms if top VCs are more likely to protect their return information. As the small size of our sample of LPAs limits our ability to study this question directly, we turn to Preqin data to test the extent to which VCs limited the disclosure of this information to their LPs. We test for limited disclosure by analyzing missing return information in Preqin, leveraging the fact that Preqin generally collects data via FOIA requests sent to public LPs. We find that after the lawsuits, the performance information of top VC firms was more likely to be missing in Preqin. This reporting gap also holds for top VCs who took money from public LPs, suggesting that some public LPs agreed to the contractual innovation and limited the scope of the information they receive from VCs. An analysis of top VC performance in MSCI-Burgiss and Preqin confirms that the latter significantly understates top VC returns due to missing data.Our final set of analyses looks at post-FOIA-shock amendments to state FOIAs that reduced public LPs’ disclosure requirements. These amendments exempted certain LPs and certain types of information from FOIA requests, which we use to explore the importance of different kinds of information. First, we document that most of the amendments focused on exempting portfolio company information, suggesting that portfolio company information was a priority for industry participants, to the extent that they shaped this regulation. A number of amendments provided protection of fund return information in addition to exempting the portfolio company data. Second, we observe a rebound in public LPs’ top VC investments following legislative protection of portfolio company information, while amendments protecting fund return information are associated with a significantly weaker restoration of prior relationships with top VC firms. Though the FOIA amendments are endogenous in their nature, these tests provide suggestive evidence that exemption of portfolio company information played an important role in the return of public LPs to top VC firms.Our paper adds to a growing literature studying institutional investors and their relationships with VC firms. First and foremost, we add to the relatively small literature that focuses on how general partners (GPs) in the PE industry assess their relationships with LPs. These papers have principally focused on LPs’ exposure to liquidity shocks. Lerner and Schoar (2004) find that GPs screen for “deep-pocket” investors by imposing restrictions on LP stake sales in investment contracts. Maurin et al. (2023) model GP preferences for illiquidity-tolerant LPs and show how they affect GPs’ behaviors. We contribute to this strand of research by studying the important but unexplored aspect of LPs’ ability to provide confidentiality. Our results demonstrate that the regulatory environment surrounding institutional investors is a relevant dimension influencing the choice of LPs by VC partnerships, going beyond the notion of access to top funds due to earlier relationships.Second, we add to the more extensive literature on LP decision-making and performance in the context of the PE industry. Lerner et al. (2007) provide the first large-scale evidence on the investment choices made by LPs; Sensoy et al. (2014) and Cavagnaro et al. (2019) explore the performance of LPs further; and Brown et al. (2021) study whether LPs can time their exposure to PE. Giommetti and Sørensen (2021), Gredil et al. (2021), Korteweg et al. (2022), and Gourier et al. (2024) study how LPs approach allocation in PE. Another strand of the literature focuses on the particular aspects of investments in PE made by public pensions, with recent contributions from Hochberg and Rauh (2012), Andonov et al. (2018), Mittal (2022), and Begenau and Siriwardane (2024). We complement this literature by demonstrating the relevance of the regulatory framework surrounding LPs, with FOIAs being an important dimension of segmentation among institutional investors. This segmentation might have implications for understanding the performance of public LPs, as they potentially face a reduced set of investment opportunities in VC relative to their private counterparts and receive less data from GPs to inform their investment decisions.Finally, our paper contributes to the literature on the costs and benefits of disclosure requirements in PE. Brown et al. (2008) propose a framework for the cost–benefit analysis of regulatory disclosure based on the introduction and quick repeal of the SEC-mandated disclosure for hedge funds. Hermalin and Weisbach (2012) argue that there could be a point beyond which additional disclosure can exacerbate agency problems and decrease firm value. By studying disclosure requirements imposed on principals (the LPs), we show that while the requirements improved information disclosure by LPs to the public, information production by agents (the GPs) for LPs has dropped.The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. In Section 2, we discuss the institutional background and the data. In Section 3, we present tests on how VCs’ sources of capital changed after the FOIA shock. In Section 4, we study implications for public LPs’ portfolio allocations and returns. In Section 5, we analyze the effects of the FOIA shock on contracts and laws. In Section 6, we conduct additional analysis and robustness tests, which further support our key findings. In Section 7, we conclude.
article.2. Institutional background and data: We first provide background information on the FOIA court rulings and the reactions of VC firms, LPs, and state legislators. We then discuss our data sources and sample.2.1. Institutional backgroundThe Enron and WorldCom scandals and the bursting of the dot-com bubble created a call for increased transparency in the early 2000s. This led not just to the Sarbanes-Oxley Act but also to generally greater scrutiny of financial information and investments. Public institutions were a natural target for disclosure advocates due to state-level open records laws, known as FOIAs, which had been enacted over the course of the 20th century. These laws set high standards of disclosure for public institutions by allowing anyone to request disclosure of previously unreleased information. FOIAs were used by journalists, watchdogs, and researchers, but also by companies and specialized business intelligence firms such as the FOIA Group. As these laws were broad, they were necessarily vague and subject to questions of interpretation. Although FOIAs offered exemptions for trade secrets, it was unclear what that meant in the context of VC.In late 2002, the potential for these laws to be used to force the disclosure of information on public LPs’ PE investments crystallized. On September 18, 2002, the University of Texas Investment Management Company (UTIMCO) responded to the choice between disclosure and litigation by making the fund-level return information available to the public.3 This unexpected change to the university’s disclosure policy triggered a chain of landmark lawsuits in late 2002, most notably against the California State Teachers’ Retirement System (CalSTRS), the California Public Employees’ Retirement System (CalPERS), and the University of California.These lawsuits were typically brought by journalists under FOIA and sought the disclosure of not just fund-level returns but also fund terms and even portfolio company information. The above-mentioned public pensions and public endowments ended up disclosing fund-level return data in late 2002 through early 2003 (Hurdle, 2005). However, uncertainty remained as to whether the ultimate scope of disclosure would expand to other data sent by VCs to their LPs such as portfolio company information.For example, a lawsuit against the nation’s largest public pension plan, CalPERS, was initiated by the San Jose Mercury News on October 25, 2002, after the pension plan refused to disclose fund-level performance data and descriptions of the portfolio companies.4 In a preliminary ruling in November 2002, the court determined that individual fund performance does not represent trade secrets, and hence is subject to disclosure under California’s FOIA. At the same time, the court left open the question of the secrecy of portfolio company data. In December 2002, CalPERS settled the case with the petitioner by agreeing to release the fund-level performance data. As part of the settlement, the portfolio company data remained private.5VC firms responded to this surprise increase in disclosure risk in different ways. Some strongly and publicly opposed disclosure. For example, Sequoia Capital not only spoke out publicly, it also expelled the University of Michigan and the University of California from its future funds and asked them to divest from its existing funds. Other VC firms took advantage of the situation. For example, the relatively young firm MPM Capital LP did not exclude CalPERS from its funds, instead saying that the firm was not concerned about return data being released and would keep a close eye on the development of the situation regarding potential portfolio company disclosures. Dennis Henner, a partner at the firm, explained, We took the FOIA issue into account and we are keeping close contact with our limiteds on the issue. We are not so concerned with our IRR being released. Our major concern is making sure that private company information stays private, but beyond that we can work with wherever this leads.6MPM Capital ended up raising one of the largest venture funds of 2002, with $900 million in its MPM Bioventures III fund. Even some of our top-listed VCs announced that they would not ban public LPs from their funds. For example, Nancy Dorman, partner at the top VC firm New Enterprise Associates, said that her firm would not discriminate against public LPs.7An important feature of these lawsuits was uncertainty regarding the scope of the required information disclosure. The plaintiffs sought comprehensive disclosure covering not just historical fund returns, but also fund terms, identities of other investors in the funds, and portfolio company information including corporate strategies, equity stakes, valuations, and financial statements. The prevailing view at the time was that disclosure of these data could lead to significant harm to startups, and by extension, their VCs.The mass release of sensitive portfolio company data ultimately never took place, but the potential for it was a recurring threat. For example, in 2004 the Texas Attorney General ruled that individual portfolio company data should be made public, before reversing that decision.8 In 2005, the Ohio Bureau of Workers’ Compensation similarly announced that it would release portfolio company performance data, before reversing course under pressure from GPs.9 Perhaps the closest case occurred in 2006, when the Ohio Department of Development disclosed information about the portfolio companies of JumpStart, an impact-focused venture firm it had contributed funds to. This disclosure led to damaging reporting on portfolio companies; for example, a local newspaper quoted JumpStart as saying “Neither the idea nor the market was strong enough to warrant further investment” in its internal reports on portfolio company CoupAd.10 Although it is challenging to assert causality, VentureSource data show patterns consistent with JumpStart suffering a reputational hit: while the firm had board seats in 42% of its portfolio companies and the average number of round participants in its deals was 2.1 before the disclosure, these numbers dropped to 3% and 1.4, respectively, after the information was made public.Several factors caused the threat of additional disclosure to lessen over time. First, the disquiet of the excluded public LP managers led state legislators to adjust their FOIAs to exclude certain information regarding PE investments of public entities, in particular the data on the underlying portfolio companies. For example, the state of Michigan amended its FOIA in 2004 to allow the University of Michigan to keep its records private. We explore these amendments in Section 5.3. Second, an absence of court- or legislation-mandated disclosure of portfolio company information gradually reduced the perceived risk of portfolio company information being subject to FOIA requests. Third, VCs and public LPs coordinated to create a limited reporting approach, in which LPs agreed to receive only selective information from VC firms, as we discuss in Section 5.1. This approach often entails carving out a class of LPs with limited information rights (named “FOIA Partner”) within the LPAs. While not every LP has accepted this approach, it has allowed some to maintain their access to top VCs. According to Buyouts Insider (2018), such restrictive clauses are now prevalent and present in every second LPA of VC funds.2.2. DataCommitments data:Preqin data (accessed via WRDS) form the basis of our primary analysis. We construct a sample of LP–GP relationships based on dated capital commitments. Our data include both the commitments themselves and a variety of LP, GP, and fund characteristics. Preqin has been extensively used by both academics (for example, Lerner et al. (2007) and Cavagnaro et al. (2019)) and practitioners focusing on the capital allocation decisions and performance of investors in PE.The FOIA shock applied only to U.S. public investors, and we limit our main sample to U.S.-based LPs to avoid potential geographic confounds. Foreign LPs account for 16% of total commitments; they are scattered across countries with different macroeconomic conditions; and their commitments might depend on LP-specific factors like currency exchange rates, prices for export commodities like oil or metals, and government policies (Bernstein et al., 2013).The distinction between U.S.-based private and public LPs is central to our analysis. State pensions constitute the majority of public LPs. Since Preqin does not distinguish between private and public endowments, we manually classify endowments as public or private, and then add the former category to the group of public investors. We further group certain state-operated sovereign wealth funds (for example, the Alaska Permanent Fund) and insurance companies (for example, the Ohio Bureau of Workers’ Compensation) as a separate category of other public LPs. We define private LPs as any non-public LPs, including, for example, private pension plans, foundations, FoF, and private endowments. This group of investors is not subject to FOIAs.We focus on U.S.-based VC funds, dropping non-U.S. funds and secondary, BO, co-investment, sidecar, and turnaround funds. We consider funds raised between 1999 and 2006 in our baseline regressions, covering about four years before and after the initial court rulings. In the analysis of potential spillover effects and capital reallocations, we extend our sample to include BO funds and FoFs. Our final sample underlying the main analysis consists of 927 LPs, which, altogether, made 6710 capital commitments to 845 VC funds managed by 511 VC firms.Performance data:We rank VC firms using VentureSource, which provides comprehensive coverage of VC portfolio companies, exits, and board memberships over that period. Following Nahata (2008), we use portfolio companies’ IPOs to identify top VCs. We rank VC firms based on VentureSource by counting the number of IPOs between 1995 and 2001 in which the VC firm participated as lead investor (with board seats). We label the top 20 VC firms in that ranking (listed in Appendix A) as top VCs.11 The list naturally includes many of the most prominent VC firms at the time. We refer to the funds raised by top VCs as top VC funds, which is an ex-ante classification based on VC firm characteristics rather than an ex-post sorting based on realized fund performance. In Section 5, we confirm the relevance of this ranking by showing that top VCs have historically delivered higher returns.In addition to capital commitments, Preqin reports the performance of VC funds. The primary source of these performance data is FOIA requests, which are directly related to the events studied in this paper (Preqin, 2023). We exploit this particular feature of this data source by exploring the absence of return information for VC funds in Preqin. Such an analysis offers important insights regarding the reaction of agents (VC firms) to the new disclosure requirements imposed on principals (public investors). As we show, the missing nature of return data on top VCs in Preqin limits our ability to reliably estimate the economic costs incurred by public LPs. Because of that, we use MSCI-Burgiss data as the basis of our return calculations. MSCI-Burgiss sources the return information directly from both public and private LPs, and hence does not suffer from bias due to the FOIA-induced changes.Descriptive statistics:Table 1 presents statistics on the types of LPs and their commitments (Panel A) as well as on the characteristics of funds raised by top VCs (Panel B) and nontop VCs (Panel C). Panel A shows that our sample contains 2060 commitments from 175 public LPs, composed of public pension funds, university endowments, and other state-operated investors. There were 4650 commitments from 722 private LPs, consisting of private pensions, foundations, FoFs, and others. Public and private pension funds together account for 45% of commitments, private foundations account for 17%, FoFs account for 16%, and insurance companies and endowments each account for 8% of commitments.Panel B explores the composition of top VC funds’ LPs immediately before the FOIA shock, immediately after it, and several years later to help gauge the long-term effects. The decline in public LPs investing in top VC funds is economically meaningful based on comparisons of the windows around the shock. The number of public LPs in top funds declines by about half, with no change in the number of private LPs. The value of public LP commitments as a share of fund size also falls by a similar magnitude. Because private LPs generally do not report the dollar values of their commitments (88% of cases in our sample), we report the value of public commitments relative to fund size rather than the ratio of public commitment value to total reported commitment value. Panel C repeats this exercise for nontop funds. While both the number and fraction of public LPs in top VC firms dramatically drops after the shock, nontop VC funds experience no real change around the event. Turning to the long-run impact measured in the 2009–2012 period, we see a statistically insignificant increase in public LP commitments to top VC funds, and a small drop in public LPs’ commitments to nontop VC funds. This is consistent with public LPs slowly returning to top VC firms.Table 1. In Panels B and C, we additionally provide fund characteristics for the 2009–2012 period. *, **, and *** indicate statistical significance at the 10%, 5%, and 1% levels, respectively.Comparing top and nontop VC funds prior to the shock, we see that relative to nontop funds, top VC funds unsurprisingly raised larger funds ($609 million vs. $265 million) and had correspondingly more LPs (19.4 vs. 7.1). Although top VCs had more public LPs (4.7 vs. 2.4), they had fewer public LPs as a portion of their investors (24% vs. 33%). This suggests that nontop VC funds may be somewhat more reliant on public investors. Table IA.1 provides additional statistics on the funds raised by top and nontop VC funds. Expectedly, top VC funds had higher sequence numbers (for example, Sequoia XI), and they were more likely to focus on early-stage investments and investments in the information technology sector.We additionally explore trends in percentages of public and private LPs with reported commitment sizes in Preqin. Since some funds do not have any public LPs or any reporting public LPs, the number of observations is lower than in our main comparisons. The statistics indicate that public LPs of nontop VC firms are initially more likely than those of top VCs to report information on commitment sizes. This wedge in reporting widens in the 2003–2006 period, as information on public LP commitments in top VC funds is about 12% more likely to be missing in Preqin after 2002. This reporting gap is primarily driven by a lower percentage of public LPs in top VCs firms reporting the data.

Supply of capital versus demand for privacyThere are several reasons why VCs might be especially privacy-conscious. First, secrecy might be a competitive advantage for the startups that the VCs themselves invest in. Past research on firm disclosure (Botosan and Stanford, 2005, Berger and Hann, 2007, Dedman and Lennox, 2009, Bernard, 2016) highlights the costs of providing excessive information to incumbent competitors, customers, and suppliers. The high uncertainty environments in which startups operate might make such disclosures especially costly for them. Second, the VC funds might face costs of disclosure, beyond damage to the startups they back. VCs’ positions could illustrate their proprietary views on markets and segments, as shown by Agarwal et al. (2013) for hedge fund managers’ confidential holdings. The staged financing model used in VC makes these concerns even more acute, as disclosing the pricing of a round or an exit could reduce a VC firm’s bargaining power in subsequent fundraising or liquidity events. Third, disclosure might impact a VC’s relationships with other LPs. The VC industry has both unusually high fees (Litvak, 2009, Metrick and Yasuda, 2011) and unusually volatile returns, a potential point of friction with union members or student stakeholders of pension funds and endowments.In Section 5 we attempt to tease out the information VCs are protecting, but for now we will focus on our finding that there is not only a general demand of VCs for privacy, but also an apparent differential response from top VCs. There are multiple possible reasons why top VCs could be more sensitive to the disclosure requirements. We group these mechanisms into two broad categories: supply-side mechanisms based on top VCs facing lower costs of turning away LPs, and demand-side mechanisms based on top VCs having an even higher demand for privacy than other funds. While an underlying demand for privacy drives the effect in both cases, the two channels are distinct based on whether the difference between top VCs and other VCs is driven by different levels of demand for privacy or different abilities to act on those desires.A supply of capital channel relies on top VC funds having excess demand from investors. The idea that top VCs are scaled lower than the willingness of investors to commit their capital is in line with Metrick and Yasuda (2010), who show that VC funds are much less scalable than BO funds. In the case of the best-performing VC funds, some but not all of this value is extracted via higher fees (Hochberg et al., 2014). By virtue of having excess demand from LPs, top VCs have a greater ability to modify their LP base in response to disclosure concerns or other selection criteria. A top VC can drop public LPs and replace them with more or larger commitments from private LPs that were eager to invest but rationed out of the funds. In contrast, lower-ranked VC firms are more reliant on their existing investor base and would have to reduce their fund size if they excluded their many public LPs from the pool of their investors. This channel makes prominent VC firms less dependent on any particular LPs and consequently more likely to forgo commitments from public LPs during the period of uncertainty following the FOIA shock.A demand for privacy channel involves top VCs having a differentially higher demand for privacy. A natural story is that high-performing VC funds may add more value and have more valuable proprietary information — if top VCs are better, their trade secrets will naturally be more valuable. Although it is challenging to rule out this story completely, the earlier results comparing VC funds’ industry specialization and their stage focus partially speak against it. Despite presumably having different needs for confidentiality, neither early-stage VCs nor those focused on certain industries show larger reactions to disclosure. As an additional test of these two channels, we look at the reactions of BO firms, which were impacted by the same disclosure shock. Top BO firms outperformed their nontop counterparts in the same manner as top VCs and so, similar to top VCs, arguably possess more valuable proprietary data than less successful BO firms. Given these similarities, top BO firms might exhibit increased demand for privacy relative to nontop BO firms. Yet, BO firms are more scalable and grow faster than VC firms (Metrick and Yasuda, 2010), which reduces the importance of the supply-side channels for this industry in general. Therefore, the analysis of top versus nontop BO firms in Table IA.3 offers a test of demand channels without substantial interference from the supply-side alternatives. Our results indicate that top BO firms show no particular reaction to these disclosure laws. The overall evidence is more consistent with a role of supply of capital channel rather than with top firms simply putting a higher value on confidentiality.Beyond potentially adding more value, top VCs differ from other VCs in terms of fees and returns. It is possible that these differences in fund terms and performance might create a higher demand for privacy that is not linked to the nature of their portfolio companies. Top VCs notably charge higher fees (Robinson and Sensoy, 2013), and the negative publicity around fee data and discrimination among LPs could harm their reputation and fundraising prospects. However, this seems unlikely for two reasons. First, Begenau and Siriwardane (2024) find that fee discounts are less common among funds in high demand, suggesting that top VCs are less likely to discriminate based on price. Second, our analysis in Section 5 suggests that VCs viewed portfolio company information as being more important. Third, it is unclear how costs of disclosing returns could be high enough to justify the dramatic shift of the LP base away from public investors. If anything, one would expect the opposite result, as top VCs persistently deliver higher returns (Harris et al., 2023), and it is unclear why disclosing good returns would be worse than disclosing poor returns.Ideally, we would also test the scaling hypothesis directly by looking at factors associated with the ability to scale. This is challenging for several reasons. First, where a fund sits relative to its optimal size is endogenous to LP commitments, and that optimal size is itself endogenous to hiring choices. Second, data on intuitive proxies of scaling (fees, subscription ratio) are usually missing for top funds. Third, our top VC measure is naturally correlated with several natural measures of ability to scale, consistent with the intuition that outperforming funds have excess demand. In line with the last point, we provide suggestive tests in Table IA.4.In Panel A, we look at several proxies for an inability to scale. First, we look at VC firms that charge carried interest above the 20% standard.14 These firms are presumably struggling to scale, as they have decided to charge higher performance fees rather than increase fund size. Second, we look at older VC firms, which are presumably more likely to have been able to scale to their preferred size. Third, we look at firms with non-core funds. Funds that have a later-stage (for example, Accel Growth) or foreign fund (for example, Accel Israel) in addition to their core fund sequence are similarly presumably struggling to scale. All of these groups see larger reductions in public LP relationships, consistent with a scalability channel. These results are in line with the findings of Robinson and Sensoy (2013), who document that carried interest is positively related to fund size and fund sequence number for VC firms. In Panel B, we look specifically at the non-core funds themselves. Unfortunately, the small number of non-core funds during this era means we lack power to find economically meaningful effects.Overall, while we cannot rule out that top VCs exhibit an increased demand for privacy relative to other VC firms, our results are more consistent with a supply of capital story, where top VCs are the only funds that have excess investor demand and so only top VCs can reallocate away from public LPs at a relatively low cost.

Changes in public LPs’ allocations an: We now turn our analysis from the VC fund-level to the LP-level, measuring how public LPs’ propensity to invest in top VCs changed and relating that back to the investment returns of public LPs.4.1. Changes in allocationsWe study how public LPs responded to being excluded from top VCs by looking at their commitments to private market funds before and after the FOIA shock. As before, we use private LPs as a control group because they were not subject to FOIA requirements. Counterfactual allocationsWe measure the direct financial effect of the FOIA shock on public LPs by comparing their actual investments to a counterfactual portfolio. Our baseline counterfactual assumes that, in the absence of the FOIA shock, public LPs would commit the same amount to all the funds raised by managers with whom they had invested previously. This reinvestment counterfactual is based on the high incidence of reinvestments within established GP–LP relationships, as documented by Lerner et al. (2007).In this analysis, we focus on the funds closed in the period from 2003 to 2006, the years immediately following the shock. We weight the actual portfolio based on LPs’ dollar commitments and the counterfactual portfolio based on the dollar value of the past commitments. We also construct count-weighted portfolios based on the number of actual or hypothetical commitments from public LPs. We consider only FoF and VC commitments, as they appear to be the margins of adjustment, and including the large number of BO commitments would add noise to the estimations. After dropping $58 billion (975) BO commitments, we start our analysis with the aggregate portfolio of $14 billion (691) VC and $12 billion (236) FoF reported commitments. Replacing the reinvestment counterfactual VC commitments with the actual public LP top VC commitments (and leaving other commitments the same) reduces the portion of top VC commitments by more than half and cuts the portfolio TVPI by 0.16× (0.31×). This dramatic drop is driven by the especially strong performance of top VCs during this period: across all of their 2003–2006 funds, top VCs delivered a TVPI of 3.4× versus 1.5× for other VCs, broadly consistent with Harris et al. (2023). The widening performance gap between top and nontop VCs in the high-uncertainty period following the FOIA shock is consistent with nontop VC firms incurring meaningful costs from the FOIA shock.Second, we measure the impact of public LPs’ nontop VC reallocations by measuring the performance difference between (a) a portfolio using actual top VC commitments and modeling other VC and FoF commitments based on the reinvestment counterfactual and (b) a portfolio using actual commitments for all VC funds and modeling FoF commitments based on the reinvestment counterfactual. The difference between these two portfolios is the effect of changed allocations to nontop VCs. These nontop VC reallocations could hurt performance if nontop VCs also excluded public LPs or if LP allocation decisions were impaired by a general reduction in their information access, as shown by Chattopadhyay et al. (2022) for public market investments. Conversely, these nontop VC reallocations could improve performance if public LPs added value by reallocating away from underperforming nontop funds. Our data show that, while the public LPs’ changes to the top VC commitments significantly decreased TVPI, their changes to nontop VC commitments increased TVPI by 0.08× (0.16×). This is consistent with the public LPs potentially adding value by reallocating among fund managers. The FOIA shock itself could plausibly even drive these effects if the reporting requirements reduced malinvestment by disciplining public investors, which now have to disclose their performance (e.g., in line with Hochberg and Rauh (2012)). Increased TVPI from nontop VC investments could also be driven by the fact that, following the event, public LPs have more information about the historical performance of the universe of VC funds.Finally, we measure the impact of public LPs’ reallocations in FoFs as the difference between (a) a portfolio where public LPs’ FoF commitments follow the reinvestment counterfactual and other commitments are based on their actual commitments and (b) public LPs’ actual commitments. The comparison shows that actual FoF investments increase TVPI by 0.03× (0.02×), consistent with public LPs’ move into top VC FoFs allowing them to recapture some of top VCs’ value creation in exchange for additional fees. Fig. 2 provides a visual representation of our analysis.The existing research on the performance of various investors in PE documents that public investors achieve lower returns in general (Lerner et al., 2007, Sensoy et al., 2014, Cavagnaro et al., 2019). We add to this literature by shedding light on potential reasons for this underperformance: disclosure requirements not only make it harder for public plans to gain access to best-performing funds but also force them out of established relationships with top VC firms. As Kaplan and Schoar (2005) point out, this persistence itself is puzzling, as in most financial markets GPs would raise fees (e.g., Berk and Green (2004)) rather than give their LPs rents. One explanation in the literature is the model proposed by Hochberg et al. (2014), where existing LPs (but not new potential LPs) know the skill of their VCs and that allows them hold up fundraising and extract information rents. In that model, the key friction is that incumbent LPs leaving a fund convey negative information. Another model is Marquez et al. (2015), where GPs choose to leave money on the table for investors to counterbalance a risk of not raising a fund or raising a small fund, both of which would be more costly for VCs with higher skill. In either model, it is not clear that ejecting public LPs would have a high cost for fund managers in conditions of credible signaling. In Marquez et al. (2015), investors are indistinguishable and competitive. In Hochberg et al. (2014), publicly ejecting LPs for a credible reason would presumably not send that manner of negative signal. In fact, Sequoia’s public scolding of the University of California would be entirely consistent with eliminating doubt as to whether its departure was based on an assessment of Sequioa’s quality or an attempt to gain bargaining power. Relatedly, we do not observe a significant drop in the fund sizes of top VC firms relative to their nontop counterparts following the event. In practice, we know that relationships in this market are sticky, and there was likely a significant cost to ejecting certain LPs from the fund. The willingness of top VCs to reshuffle their investors confirms that VCs do indeed put high value on privacy.
article.5. Contractual and regulatory innovation: The disclosure shock led to compensating changes in both the contracts between LPs and VCs and the FOIA laws themselves. We first document changes in LPAs around the FOIA shock, highlighting what information that VCs and LPs agreed to protect and the consequences of that information protection for public information availability. We then examine the FOIA amendments passed in several states, documenting the information they targeted and the effects of these confidentiality-enhancing legal interventions.5.1. Evolution of contracts between VCs and LPsThe rights and responsibilities of VCs and their LPs are spelled out in lengthy LPAs. Consistent with the general secrecy of the industry, these documents themselves are confidential and not generally available. However, we collect a sample of these investment contracts from anonymous data providers. We consider two samples of LPAs: a broad sample of 39 LPAs from 1999–2006 and a matched sample of 14 LPAs created by taking the latest pre-FOIA LPA and the earliest post-FOIA LPA for the seven VC firms. Fig. 3 summarizes the changes in these contracts around the FOIA shock, with points farther to the right denoting higher fraction of LPAs offering a particular protection. We see a significant strengthening of confidentiality requirements in both samples. Most pre-FOIA shock LPAs did not have a confidentiality clause at all, while every firm in both samples had such a clause following the FOIA shock. These clauses gave VCs broad control over information disclosure, often allowing VCs to anonymize and redact data or provide information access only temporarily via a password protected online portal. None of the pre-FOIA LPAs were themselves marked as confidential, while three-quarters of the post-FOIA LPAs were. None of the pre-FOIA LPAs and almost all of the post-FOIA LPAs contained terms allowing VCs to provide different information to different LPs — with the LPs excluded from information access being often explicitly referred to as “Public Fund Partners”, “FOIA Partners”, or similar. Section IA.2 of Online Appendix provides an example of such a clause. As we discuss in Section 6.1, we also see that some fund managers adopted clauses preventing transfers of LP interests to public LPs in the secondary market.Download: Download high-res image (548KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 3. The x-axis shows the percentage of LPAs offering a particular protection, with triangles (circles) marking the frequency of a protection before (after) to the FOIA shock.Turning to the type of information protected, LPAs consistently added broad protections for portfolio company information. We report two metrics: first, whether LPAs do not explicitly allow disclosure (with high numbers corresponding to the absence of terms allowing disclosure) and second, whether LPAs explicitly prohibited disclosure (with high numbers corresponding to the presence of terms prohibiting disclosure). Disclosing portfolio company information was rarely specifically prohibited prior to the FOIA shock, but after the shock all LPAs in the sample explicitly barred the disclosure of sensitive information related to specific startups. One LPA allowed the disclosure of company names, locations, and broad lines of business, which we interpret as protecting portfolio company information. This analysis paints a consistent picture of LPAs providing extensive protection to portfolio company information after the FOIA shock.While portfolio company information was uniformly protected, most VCs explicitly allowed fund-level return information disclosure. Some VCs did prohibit any return information disclosure, but the general pattern is much weaker than that seen with the protection of portfolio company information. The investment contracts are, of course, the result of negotiations between VCs and their LPs and a by-product of the needs and bargaining power of both sides. However, the fact that portfolio company data was more protected than fund-level performance information suggests that the VCs saw the confidentiality of portfolio company information as of the highest importance.The limitations to the data transmission affected every second investment contract in different forms (Buyouts Insider, 2018) and left many public LPs with less information. On the upside, these new clauses could have allowed public LPs to preserve or regain relationships with VC firms. On the downside, however, terms restricting information access by public LPs make both VC and aggregate allocation decisions more challenging and raise legal and compliance concerns. Higher information asymmetry faced by public LPs might also undermine their performance in the secondary market for LP stakes (Nadauld et al., 2019) as well as co-investments with fund managers (Fang et al., 2015, Braun et al., 2020).Missing returns datapoints in fund-level performance reports of public LPs are a testament to added restrictions on information sharing and disclosure. For example, the 2017 Report on Alternative Investment Returns by the Regents of the University of California reports that they were “unable to provide fund level information [for Kleiner Perkins and Sequoia Capital] because The Regents of the University of California does not receive this information from the General Partner” (see Section IA.4 of Online Appendix). Though the top VCs in our sample were more likely to add terms protecting against disclosure, unfortunately, our small sample of investment contracts prevents us from making generalizations. To show broader evidence on the impact of these contractual terms and test whether there was a differential impact on top VCs, we turn to missing return information in Preqin.5.2. Evidence from missing returnsWe shed light on the prevalence of data-protecting LPAs by studying an outcome of these restrictions: missing return data in Preqin. While parts of the data in Preqin are collected via voluntary disclosures by GPs and private LPs, routine FOIA requests sent to public LPs are the dominant source of return data in the database. Because of this, Preqin systematically collects return data using FOIA requests, and, under current FOIA laws, public investors must generally report performance information in response to these requests. This means that the returns of a fund with public commitments can be missing from Preqin when the public LP is not able to send data to Preqin as the LP does not possess the requested data due to limited reporting. At the same time, the return data of funds without public LPs can be present due to sporadic reporting by private investors or direct (and potentially selective) reporting by funds themselves.As our first step, we analyze the absence of return information in Preqin to ascertain whether the lawsuits led to significant changes in information transmission between public LPs and VC funds. Specifically, we define return information as missing if a particular VC fund does not have a single return data point in Preqin. As the presence of return information is a characteristic of a fund, we perform this analysis at the VC fund-level. We extend our sample period and consider ten years before and after the lawsuits to address the slow-moving and long-term nature of the evolution of investment contracts in response to the FOIA shock. As we are most interested in capturing cases when GPs did not send information to their public LPs, we additionally interact our key variable of interest with the presence of at least one public LP in the fund.Our tests are based on the following regression specification: (4)ɛYi=β1Postt×TopFirmi+β2Xi+YearFEt+ɛi.where the outcome variable of interest, Yi, is the absence of return data in Preqin for fund i raised in year t. This result points to a return reporting gap for top VC firms in Preqin. The overall scope of the limited disclosure framework is likely to be much larger, as we observe only one particular – and arguably one of the most stringent – restriction concerning the basic fund-level return information. These costs were driven by the issues disclosure creates for VC funds and, we argue, for the portfolio companies in which those VCs invest. This type of disclosure costs complement findings on mutual funds (Agarwal et al., 2015), hedge funds (Shi, 2017), and innovative public firms (Breuer et al., 2022). To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to report the deleterious impact of a VC firm’s investors disclosing information.Although public LPs, their VCs, and those VCs’ portfolio companies face direct costs of disclosure, the general equilibrium effects are unclear. While some VCs excluded public LPs and amended contracts to reduce information sharing and data disclosure, there is now more publicly available information on both VC firms and LP investments. As documented for public company (Badertscher et al., 2013) and fund manager (Agarwal et al., 2015) disclosure, VC disclosure could have spillovers for industry players in different directions. VC firms could learn the performance of their competitors and form preferences regarding the choice of syndication partners. Startups could target specific VCs based on their prior performance, fund sizes, or LP base. LPs might be better able to benchmark performance across funds and benefit from more liquid secondary markets (as we discuss in Section 6.1). Although these general equilibrium effects are compelling to explore, they are often challenging to test within our empirical framework. For example, while the case of Jumpstart (discussed in Section 2.1) is consistent with VC firms carefully choosing their investment partners, testing the broader effects of disclosure risks on syndication patterns requires establishing the links between startups and VC funds (rather than VC firms). In Table IA.6, we conduct tests on the propensity of top VCs syndicating with nontop VCs at the VC firm level and do not detect meaningful changes. The lack of evidence might be due to the measurement error and the associated attenuation bias, as investment rounds we observe in the post-treatment period are often done by the funds raised prior to the FOIA shock. In addition, the spread of FOIA amendments and changes in LPAs may have weakened the effect, even though their passage and effectiveness were unclear ex-ante at the time of the initial court rulings.5.3. Regulatory innovationSeveral states amended their FOIAs following the FOIA shock. These amendments clarified and restricted the scope of public investors’ required disclosure. Unlike the initial lawsuits, the amendments did not come as a shock to the VC industry participants. These law changes were passed with the explicit goal of helping public LPs regain access to VC funds, presumably based on lobbying and consultation from VC and instate public LPs. For example, the California senate bill exempting VC returns from FOIA requests reads “Following recent litigation seeking to require public pension funds and retirement systems and public endowments or foundations to disclose certain information about alternative investments, the funds risk being excluded from participation in certain alternative investments”. Although this endogeneity makes causal analysis challenging, it opens another avenue of exploration: to the extent VCs and LPs drove these amendments, the nature of the amendments reveals what the VC industry saw as important.We exploit the differences in information protected by these amendments to shed light on the causes of disclosure avoidance. Fig. 4 shows the 14 states that passed FOIA amendments between 2002 and 2012 and summarizes the nature of those amendments. Section IA.3 of Online Appendix reproduces their text. Consistent with portfolio company information being the key point of contention, all the amendments protect portfolio company information. About a third of states additionally protected fund-level return information and stated that LPs need only disclose asset-class level returns. These protections sometimes applied to all public LPs in a state, and sometimes exempted only certain LPs (e.g., the University of Washington’s endowment). article.6. Additional analysis and robustness te: This section contains three sets of additional analysis. We first look at the evolution of the secondary market for LP stakes. Next, we document changes in fund composition and size and relate those to VC quality. Finally, we show that our results are robust to considering reinvestment decisions, adding foreign LPs, different definitions of top VC, and considering only VCs with pre-existing relationships with public investors.6.1. The secondary market for LP stakesThe FOIA shock could impact the secondary market for LP stakes in several ways. The increased public disclosure of fund information could increase liquidity and transfers by improving matching and fund evaluation (e.g., Bhide (1993)), but the reduction in VCs’ information sharing with public LPs could increase information asymmetry and harm liquidity. VCs that prevented public LPs from buying fund stakes would reduce the number of potential buyers and reduce liquidity, but if VCs were able to force public LPs to sell existing fund stakes, we might see more sales after the initial court rulings. All of these channels would be mediated by VCs’ bargaining power.To shed some light on potential changes regarding the secondary transactions in LP stakes, we use Pitchbook data which contains some limited information on secondary commitments made by LPs (we observe buyers only). Figure IA.2 plots the frequency of secondary transactions of LP stakes in top and nontop VC funds. We see a significant increase in trading of nontop VC fund stakes starting in 2003 and no corresponding increase in the trading of top VC fund stakes. This is consistent with the greater supply of information making nontop VC stakes more transferable. With that said, the secondary markets were only just emerging in the early parts of the sample (as Nadauld et al. (2019) report) and our data are incomplete.Turning to our LPAs, we find that the VCs that restricted public LPs’ information access also ensured that public LPs were not able to buy fund stakes on the secondary market. Although most LPAs commonly required GPs’ consent for transfers even before the FOIA shock, there was a pronounced tightening in transfer rules after the shock. Out of our seven VC firms that raised funds before and after the shock, two added language explicitly banning transfers to entities subject to FOIA requests. Another fund allowed transfers without GP consent prior to the shock, but it added a VC consent requirement immediately after. These contractual changes provide additional evidence that the FOIA shock impacted the secondary transactions in LP interests. While Lerner and Schoar (2004) find that transfer restrictions can serve as a choice parameter to screen for deep-pocket investors, we show that these clauses could also be tailored to protect the fund from becoming exposed to disclosure requirements.6.2. Fund size and investor demandOur main results indicate that top VC firms reduced the fraction of commitments from public LPs following the lawsuits. As public LPs accounted for 24% of total commitments in an average top VC fund prior to the shock (Panel B of Table 1), this reallocation might interfere with VCs’ fundraising process and ultimately result in smaller fund sizes. We test this possibility using fund-level tests. While the estimations in Table IA.7 point to a decrease in the size of funds raised by top VCs relative to a control group of lower-ranked VCs after the disclosure, this change is not statistically significant. This observation is consistent with the view that top VCs can more easily substitute their public LP base with more or larger commitments from private LPs and therefore mitigate the adverse impact of reallocations. Smaller and less successful VC firms might experience great difficulties in replacing their LPs, and they are in general more dependent on their existing LP base. Relatedly, Chen and Ewens (2021) document that the regulatory limitations on banks’ ability to commit capital to VC funds led to a decline in fund sizes of exposed local VC firms and hurt their future fundraising activity.Since the drop in the fraction of commitments from public LPs might be driven by an increase in the number of investments from private LPs, we additionally estimate regressions for the number of LPs. The estimates in columns 2 to 4 of Table IA.7 suggest that the reallocations are mostly driven by the drop in the number of public LPs. The change in the number of private LPs is positive but not particularly pronounced. While the data limitations do not allow us to explore the changes in commitment sizes directly, the absence of changes in fund sizes of top VC firms could potentially stem from private LPs making larger commitments to this category of VCs.Table IA.8 additionally examines fund size and investor demand for funds raised by top VC and BO firms. In Panel A, we show that top VCs raise larger funds and have higher subscription ratios, consistent with these firms being higher quality and capacity constrained. Panel B indicates that funds raised by top BO firms are also larger, but they are not more likely to be oversubscribed — consistent with only top VCs having excess demand. These tests are based on an extended time period as we lack the data on subscription ratios for the majority of funds.6.3. Robustness checksForeign LPs as an additional control group. As Table IA.9 shows, our results are mostly unchanged if we add foreign LPs to our control group. Our main coefficient of interest on the fund-level fraction of public commitments moves only slightly — from 0.147 to 0.142. Columns 3 to 6 also show that foreign LPs do not explain the absence of significant change in fund sizes of top VC firms: following the FOIA shock, top VCs increased their reliance on U.S.-based private LPs rather than foreign LPs. Table IA.10 reveals similar patterns in terms of summary statistics. We exclude foreign LPs from our main analysis because of the worries about potential LP geography confounds, which we can address within the sample of U.S.-based LPs by inclusion of granular LP state-by-year fixed effects.Reinvestments. In this test, we model reinvestments by considering both the new commitments and the terminations of connections, defined as the absence of a commitment from a previously investing LP in a given VC firm’s newly raised fund. We follow the prior literature on the skill of LPs by focusing on their reinvestment decisions in PE (Lerner et al., 2007, Sensoy et al., 2014, Cavagnaro et al., 2019). We perform this analysis separately for public and private LPs due to the concern that the disaggregated commitment-level tests could be especially sensitive to the sporadic nature of known investment links for private LPs. Table IA.11 confirms that following the lawsuits, top VCs are less likely to accept reinvestments from public LPs. Reinvestments by private LPs show a smaller overall adjustment, though the propensity to reinvest grows during the first year following the disclosure shock.Varying definitions of top VC. In Table IA.12, we separately consider the top 10, 20, 30, and 40 VC firms. The baseline results stay strong as we limit our sample of affected firms to the top 10, with the coefficients shrinking as we use broader definitions of top VCs. This is consistent with lower ranked VCs being less able to react to the LP disclosure requirements.Restricting sample to VCs with prior public LP relationships. Table IA.13 shows that our key results persist after eliminating VCs with no prior links to public LPs, as top VCs shift from their existing connections with FOIA-exposed plans to private LPs. This observation not only mitigates the concern about inherently different types of LPs, but also reinforces the main argument that performance disclosure by public LPs is the key driver of the changes.
article.7. Conclusion: We study the impact of disclosure on capital allocation decisions in the context of the VC industry. We show that a strengthening of FOIA-based disclosure requirements for public investors led many top VCs to exclude these investors from their funds. At the same time, top VCs increase the fraction of commitments from their existing private LPs and also FoFs.The reallocations significantly impaired the performance of public LPs’ VC investments, highlighting the value of confidentiality in VC investing and the trade-off between performance and transparency. Specifically, lost access to top VCs reduced public investor returns by $4.3 billion compared to the naive reinvestment counterfactual. Reallocations among nontop VCs and using FoFs to regain access to top VCs helped public LPs offset most of the performance loss, reducing losses to $1.6 billion.It is natural to ask what type of information top VCs sought to protect by excluding public plans from their investor base. To inform our analysis, we explore the evolution of investment contracts and changes in FOIA amendments following the FOIA shock. Overall, we see a strong and consistent pattern: the language of LPAs was overwhelmingly changed to protect portfolio company information rather than to protect fund return information. Similarly, state amendments focused on exempting portfolio company data from the scope of FOIAs, with some states also protecting fund-level information. Using the state-level heterogeneity in law content, we provide suggestive evidence that exempting portfolio company information from disclosure was more effective in restoring relationships between top VCs and public plans.A long-term and pervasive consequence of these disclosure requirements is a reduction in the amount of information VCs share with their public LPs. We demonstrate this channel by looking at the completeness of return information in Preqin. Top VC funds, including funds with public LPs among their investors, are less likely to have their return data available after the regulation. While the direct impact of the lawsuits is limited to a fraction of each LP’s portfolio invested in top VCs, the systematic lack of information on fund performance and other information on fund operations represents an additional information cost, as public LPs have less information to use to compare performance across funds when choosing new fund managers and making reinvestment decisions. As the industry participants themselves point out, FOIAs present a barrier to giving investors the information they need (Pennsylvania’s Public Pension Management and Asset Investment Review Commission, 2018).Beyond the specific case of public LPs’ disclosure policies, our results underscore the cost of legal uncertainty. Further, they show how endogenous changes in information can counteract policy in a multi-layer principal–agent framework. Forcing principals (plan managers) to disclose information created limited transparency as it led their agents (VC funds) to share less information with them.
